ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vegan mutton masala; 'curry dish' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest defensible parent class. Vegan/plant-based mutton masala is a subcategory of spiced curry dishes.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat substitute dishes — spiced curry preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for vegan mutton masala. A0FJP (meat substitute dishes) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Spiced curry preparation qualifier applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for vegan mutton masala or plant-based curry dishes as of 2026.

main_ingredients: plant-based meat substitute (soy or wheat gluten)
allergens: gluten/wheat (likely, depending on plant-based protein used)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Vegan Mutton Masala in Singapore is approximately 200g, equivalent to one medium bowl or a side portion served alongside rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Vegan Mutton Masala — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Vegan Mutton Masala (cr_id G00010865) with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.0g/100g based on typical plant-based curry fat profile; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat data not reported by HPB for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Spiced Legume and Plant-Protein Curry Dishes — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vegan Mutton Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous spiced plant-protein curry dishes with similar carbohydrate content (~7–10g/100g), high protein (~12–15g/100g), and moderate fat (~10–12g/100g). The low carbohydrate load (7.44g/100g), high protein content, and presence of anti-glycaemic spices (turmeric, ginger, cumin) support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 5.2g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g; a 200g serving delivers 934mg sodium, representing approximately 41% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100g or less per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as pickles, soy sauce, or processed condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 7.44g/100g with sugar at 4.69g/100g; protein at 14.05g/100g from plant-based substitute moderates postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegan Mutton Masala is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate content and high plant protein; patients should avoid pairing with large portions of white rice or naan and verify that the plant-based protein substitute does not contain added starch or sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.58g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~4.0g/100g; sodium 467mg/100g is the primary cardiovascular risk factor; cholesterol reported at 85mg/100g which is atypical for a vegan dish and warrants verification.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content (467mg/100g) and verify the cholesterol value of 85mg/100g with the manufacturer of the plant-based protein substitute, as true vegan dishes should contain negligible cholesterol; request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion to 100–150g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vegan formulation avoids arachidonic acid from red meat; turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols) provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; however sodium at 467mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile of this dish but should request reduced-salt preparation, monitor for nightshade sensitivity (tomatoes, red chilli), and verify that the plant-based protein substitute does not contain pro-inflammatory additives or trans fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegan Mutton Masala is a plant-based adaptation of the classic South Asian mutton masala curry, popular in Singapore's multicultural food landscape. Instead of traditional mutton (goat meat), this dish uses a plant-based meat substitute — typically made from soy protein, wheat gluten (seitan), or textured vegetable protein — cooked in a richly spiced masala sauce. The sauce is built on a base of onions, tomatoes, garlic, and ginger, seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, red chilli, and garam masala, then simmered until the protein absorbs the aromatic gravy. The result is a deeply flavoured, moderately spicy curry with a tender, meaty texture from the plant-based substitute. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05g protein, 11.58g fat, and only 7.44g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish suitable for diabetes management. Sodium is elevated at 467mg/100g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger provide additional functional health benefits. This dish is fully vegan and dairy-free, and is commonly served at lunch or dinner alongside steamed rice, roti prata, or naan in Singapore's Indian-Muslim and vegetarian restaurant settings.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00010865) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. Saturated fat is estimated from typical plant-based curry fat profiles as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for Vegan Mutton Masala are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this entry (G00010865) as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat estimated at 4.0g/100g based on typical plant-based curry fat composition; this is not directly reported by HPB. 3. Cholesterol is reported at 85mg/100g in the HPB source, which is atypical for a fully vegan dish; this may reflect the plant-based protein substitute used or a data anomaly — downstream users should verify with the product manufacturer. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable spiced plant-protein curry dishes; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. FoodEx2 code A0FJP and FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for vegan mutton masala as of 2026. 6. All core macros (protein_g 14.05, fat_g 11.58, carbohydrate_g 7.44) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
